segments.purgatorio-24-g21: Sì here functions as 'thus' or 'so' (not a simple 'yes'); disse is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of dire, meaning 'he said'; prima is an adverb 'before/earlier'. Together the clause states that he had already said this previously.
segments.purgatorio-24-g22: e poi is a simple coordinating phrase 'and then' (e = 'and', poi = 'then/afterwards'); the colon that follows marks the introduction of direct speech (what he says next).
segments.purgatorio-24-g23: Qui = 'here' (adverb). Non si vieta is an impersonal or passive construction with si + 3rd-sg vowel (vieta, present indicative) meaning 'it is not forbidden' (literally 'one does not forbid' / 'is not forbidden'). Di introduces the following infinitive; nominar is the archaic/poetic infinitive form (modern nominare) meaning 'to name' or 'to mention'. Ciascun (archaic short form of ciascuno) is the object 'each one.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g24: Da ch’ is the contracted form of da che, used causally/temporally meaning 'since' or 'now that'. È (present indicative of essere) with the past participle munta forms a perfect idea ('has been munta'); munta is a past participle used figuratively here from the sense 'milked/drained' (i.e. wasted away). Sì = 'so' (intensifier). Nostra sembianza = 'our appearance/likeness.' Via is used adverbially here ('away'); per introduces the agent or cause 'by/through' and la dïeta ('the diet/fast')—the diëresis on ï marks the syllabification for the verse. The whole subordinate phrase explains the reason: since our appearance has been so drained away by the fast.
segments.purgatorio-24-g25: Questi is a demonstrative pronoun meaning “this one / this man.” It stands alone as the identification the speaker gives before the following action; in dialogue it functions like a vocative/pointing remark.
segments.purgatorio-24-g26: e is the coordinating conjunction “and,” mostrò is passato remoto (third person singular) of mostrare meaning “he showed/pointed out,” and col is the contraction of con + il “with the.” dito is the instrument “finger.” Together: “and he pointed with the finger.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g27: «è is the present indicative third person singular of essere, used in direct speech to identify someone (“is”), and Bonagiunta is the proper name being predicated by that verb: “is Bonagiunta.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g28: This is an appositive giving origin: the name repeated (Bonagiunta) followed by da + place (da Lucca) — da means “from” and Lucca is the town. The semicolon ends the identification phrase.
segments.purgatorio-24-g29: This whole phrase is inverted: quella faccia di là da lui (“that face yonder from him”) is the item being described; più che l’altre trapunta is a comparative modifier meaning “more adorned/embellished than the others” (trapunta is a past participle agreeing with faccia). The verb and subject follow: ebbe la Santa Chiesa in le sue braccia — literally “the Holy Church had [it] in her arms.” So the order is stylistic inversion: the thing (the face) is fronted and then we read that the Church held it in her arms.
segments.purgatorio-24-g30: dal is the contraction da + il (“from the”), Torso is a name or house-name, and fu is passato remoto third singular of essere (“was”/“came from”). The phrase states origin/affiliation: “he was from the Torso.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g31: e is “and”; purga is present indicative third person singular of purgare and here describes a penitential/abstinent action (“he purges/abstains”); per + digiuno means “by/through fasting” (per indicates the means); l’anguille di Bolsena e la vernaccia are the items abstained from (the eels of Bolsena and the vernaccia wine). The clause thus says he purges himself by fasting from those things.
segments.purgatorio-24-g32: Molti altri = “many others.” mi is the indirect object “to me,” and nomò is passato remoto third person singular of nominare (“named”). ad uno ad uno is a repeated prepositional phrase meaning “one by one” (ad = to). The whole line: “Many others he named to me, one by one.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g33: e = “and”; del is the contraction di + il (“of the” or “at the” depending on sense) and introduces nomar (an infinitive used here as a noun, “the naming”); parean is an archaic form of parevano/sembravano (third plural), meaning “they seemed”; tutti = “all”; contenti = “content/pleased.” Together: “and at the naming they all seemed content.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g34: sì ch’ = sì che, a resultive conjunction “so that”; io is the subject “I”; però here links result (“therefore/for that reason”); non vidi is passato remoto negative “I did not see”; un atto bruno = “a dark/gloomy gesture” (atto = gesture/act, bruno = dark/gloomy). The clause expresses result: “so that I did not see a dark gesture.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g35: Vidi is first-person passato remoto “I saw.” The clause contains an object + infinitive construction: Vidi (them) usar li denti = “I saw (them) use their teeth,” where usar (infinitive) takes li denti as its object. per fame expresses cause (“through/for hunger”), and a vòto = “in vain.” The names Ubaldin da la Pila e Bonifazio are the people seen; che introduces a relative clause modifying (the latter name or both) with past historic pasturò (“he/she pastured/led”) and col rocco (“with the crozier/rod”) many people (molte genti) as direct object. Grammatically: main verb vidi + accusative/infinitive clause + appended relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-24-g51: Femmina è nata places the noun before the verb for emphasis. È nata is essere + past participle (literally “is born”) used idiomatically to state that someone has been born (perfect/resultative sense). The word order and the use of nata mark a completed birth-state rather than a progressive action.
segments.purgatorio-24-g52: e non porta ancor benda is a negative present statement: porta is present indicative of portare meaning “wear” here, with non and the archaic ancor (ancora) = “yet/still.” Benda is a head-covering/fillet; the clause says she does not yet wear one.
segments.purgatorio-24-g53: cominciò el uses the passato remoto (cominciò) “he began,” common in narrative, followed by the archaic subject pronoun el (egli) after the verb — an inversion frequent in older Italian poetic prose and verse.
segments.purgatorio-24-g54: che introduces a relative clause referring back to “Femmina.” Ti farà piacere la mia città is an idiomatic structure with fare + piacere: farà (future of fare) and piacere meaning “to please,” with ti as the indirect object — “my city will give you pleasure / will please you.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g55: come ch’om la riprenda is a concessive clause: come = “however/as,” ch’ = che, and om = generic “one/people.” Riprenda is present subjunctive of riprendere used here to express an indefinite or potential judgement (“may judge/criticize”). The whole phrase means “no matter how people may take/criticize it.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g56: Tu is the subject “you”; te is the reflexive/clitic “yourself”; n’ is the clitic ne (here marking departure: andarsene = to go away), and andrai is future second person singular of andare (“you will go”). con questo antivedere = “with this foreknowledge/anticipation.” Together: “You yourself will go away with this foreseeing.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g57: se = “if”; nel is the contraction in + il (“in the”); mio mormorar is a noun phrase “my murmuring/whispering”; prendesti is an archaic or literary past form (second person singular passato) of prendere, here “you have taken/assumed” (i.e., “if you have mistaken/taken [an error]”); errore is the object “an error/mistake.” The clause means “if in my murmuring you took/assumed an error.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g58: dichiareranti is an archaic/poetic future form meaning “they will declare/to you” (in Dante the clitic may be incorporated in the form); ancor = ancora = “still/again”; le cose vere = “the true things.” The clause promises that (if you erred) they will again declare the true matters to you. Grammatically, it is a future indicative with an adverb and a direct object.
segments.purgatorio-24-g59: This is a single clause introduced by the adversative Ma (“But”). dì is the imperative “say/tell” addressing someone; s’i’ is a contraction for se io (“if I”) introducing an indirect question (here rendered “whether I”); veggio is 1st-person present “I see”; qui is locative “here”; colui che is the demonstrative + relative “the one who”; fore is an archaic/poetic form meaning outside/away (here spatial/figurative) and links to trasse; trasse is the passato remoto (he drew/produced) used here as a reported past action; le rime is the direct object “the (nine) rhymes.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g60: cominciando is a present participle/gerund “beginning,” linking the quoted opening line to the preceding clause; the quoted line is given in full: Donne is the vocative/nominative “Women”; ch’ (che) introduces the relative clause “who/that”; avete is 2nd-person plural present “you have”; intelletto is “understanding/intellect”; d’ (di) is the preposition “of”; amore is “love.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g61: A short speech tag: E io a lui literally “And I to him,” marking that the speaker turns to address the addressee. a is the preposition “to,” and lui is the indirect-object pronoun “him.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g62: «I’ mi son un che, is a self-identifying formula: I’ (io) = “I,” mi is a clitic emphasizing the subject, son is the 1st-person of essere (here archaic son = sono, “I am”), un che = “one who” (a man who / someone who), introducing a relative clause describing the speaker’s habitual behaviour.
segments.purgatorio-24-g63: quando Amor mi spira is a temporal clause: quando = “when”; Amor is personified and capitalized (Love as an agent); mi is the object clitic “me”; spira is 3rd-person singular present of spirare, here “breathes into/inspires,” so “when Love inspires me.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g64: noto functions here as a verb in sense “I note/record/mark” (1st-person present), meaning that when inspired the speaker registers or sets down what he receives; it is elliptical and concise as typical in Dante’s style.
segments.purgatorio-24-g65: e a quel modo ch’e’ ditta dentro vo significando is a linked clause explaining method: e = “and”; a quel modo = “in that manner”; ch’ = relative “which/that”; e’ is the archaic/typographic form of è “is” (copula) introducing ditta (past participle ‘dictated’) meaning “which is dictated within”; dentro = “within”; vo (from andare: vo) = “I go / I proceed” (used idiomatically to mean “I proceed”); significando is a present participle “signifying/explaining,” so the whole = “and in that manner which is dictated within I proceed to explain.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g1: Né is the coordinating negative 'nor'; dir is the infinitive used substantively ('the saying'/'speaking'); l’ is the elided definite article 'il'; andar is another infinitive-noun ('the going'/'the walking'). Together they form a negative pair: 'neither the saying nor the walking'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g2: Né again 'nor'; l’ andar is the definite infinitive-substantive 'the going'; lui is the clitic pronoun 'him' (object) referring back to the person whose pace is under discussion; più lento 'slower'; facea is the imperfect of fare ('was making'/'made'). The clause means 'nor did the walking make him any slower.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g3: ma = 'but'; ragionando is a present gerund ('reasoning'/'talking') that links to the subject of the main verb; andavam is the first-person plural imperfect of andare ('we were going' / 'we went' in narrative); forte is used adverbially ('quickly/with force'). The construction describes simultaneous action: 'but, reasoning, we went quickly.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g4: sì come = 'just as' (introducing a simile); nave 'ship'; pinta is a past participle/adjective from pingere ('painted' or poetically 'trimmed/bright'), modifying nave; da marks agency/means here ('by'); buon vento = 'a good wind'. The image: 'like a painted ship driven by a good wind.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g5: e = 'and'; l’ (il) the article; ombre 'shades'/'ghostly spirits' (the souls Dante sees). This simply introduces the subject 'and the shades'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g6: che introduces a relative clause ('which'); parean is the imperfect of parere ('seemed'); cose 'things'; rimorte (rimorto) is a poetic adjective meaning literally 'dead again' or 'lifeless/pale' — here 'seeming like half-dead things.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g7: per = 'through'/'by means of'; le fosse 'the hollows/pits' (here the eye-sockets); de li = of the (poetic word order, de + li = dei/degli); occhi 'eyes'; ammirazione 'astonishment/wonder' (what is being drawn); traean is the imperfect third-person plural of trarre ('they drew/pulled'); di me = 'from me.' The clause: 'through the hollows of their eyes they drew astonishment from me.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g8: di = 'of/from'; mio 'my'; vivere here is a noun meaning 'living' or 'the fact of being alive' (one's life/conduct); accorte is an adjective (feminine plural) agreeing with ombre, meaning 'aware' or 'mindful.' So literally 'aware of my living' — they were aware of (took notice of) my being alive.
segments.purgatorio-24-g9: E io = 'And I' (simple conjunction + pronoun). It marks the speaker's turn to speak.
segments.purgatorio-24-g10: continüando is the present gerund 'continuing' (note the diaeresis indicating pronunciation/metrical break); al mio sermone = 'to my discourse' (al = a + il). The gerund links this continuing action to the main verb that follows.
segments.purgatorio-24-g11: dissi is the passato remoto (simple past) first-person singular of dire: 'I said.' It introduces reported speech (the quoted speech that follows).
segments.purgatorio-24-g12: Ella = 'she' (subject); va = present of andare 'goes'; sù = 'up'; forse = 'perhaps'; più tarda = 'slower/more slowly'; che non farebbe is a negative comparative construction meaning 'than she would (do)' — non is used in the comparison and farebbe is the conditional 'would make/do.' The line expresses a comparative: 'she goes up perhaps more slowly than she would.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g13: per = 'because of'/'for'; altrui = 'others'/'someone else's'; cagione = 'cause'/'reason.' The short prepositional phrase gives the reason: 'because of someone else's cause' (i.e., on account of others).
segments.purgatorio-24-g14: Ma = 'but'; dimmi is the imperative (tell me) 2nd-person singular with clitic 'mi'. It introduces a request for information.
segments.purgatorio-24-g15: se tu sai = 'if you know': se introduces a conditional subordinate clause; tu is the explicit subject 'you' (emphatic); sai is the present indicative 'you know.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g16: dov’ is the contracted form of dove ('where'); è is present indicative of essere 'is'; Piccarda is a proper name. The clause asks for Piccarda's location: 'where is Piccarda?'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g17: dimmi = 'tell me' (imperative); s’io is an archaic contraction of se io ('if I' / 'whether I'); veggio here is used in the sense 'I can see'/'I perceive' (archaic use); da notar persona = 'to note/observe a person' (da + infinitive expresses purpose/ability); tra questa gente = 'among these people'; che sì mi riguarda = 'who so concerns me' (che = who/that; sì = so/in such a way; mi riguarda = 'concerns me'). The whole: 'tell me whether I perceive any person among these people who concerns me so.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g18: La mia sorella = 'my sister': definite article + possessive + noun. Simple nominative phrase introducing the person named.
segments.purgatorio-24-g19: che = 'who/that' (relative pronoun); tra bella e buona = 'between beautiful and good' (comparing two qualities); non so = 'I do not know' (negated present of sapere); qual fosse più uses the imperfect subjunctive fosse because of the indefinite/unknown relative ('which was more'), and più = 'more.' The clause expresses Dante's inability to decide whether she was more beautiful or more good.
segments.purgatorio-24-g20: trïunfa is present indicative 'triumphs' (the dieresis marks the syllabic division tri-ün-fa for the meter); lieta = 'joyful' modifies the subject; ne l’alto Olimpo = 'in the high Olympus' (ne = in); già = 'already'; di sua corona = 'with her crown' (di here marks possession/attainment). The clause: 'she triumphs, joyful, already in high Olympus with her crown.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g123: Vidi is the passato remoto (simple past) 'I saw'. 'gente' is a collective noun (people). 'sott’' is the contracted poetic form of 'sotto' before a vowel; 'esso' is a third-person neuter pronoun 'it'. 'alzar le mani' uses the infinitive alzar(e) after a verb of perception (Vidi ... alzar) meaning 'I saw ... raise', with the definite article 'le' marking 'mani' (hands). 'e gridar non so che' puts an infinitive (gridar) coordinated to alzar and uses the impersonal expression 'non so che' = 'I know not what' (literally 'I do not know what [they shout]'). 'verso le fronde' is a directional phrase 'toward the leaves/foliage.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g124: 'quasi' here means 'as if' or 'like'. 'bramosi' is an adjective in the plural agreeing with 'fantolini' and means 'longing/eager'. 'fantolini' is a diminutive plural (little boys or little pages); the following adjective 'vani' is coordinate, 'vain' or frivolous. The relative clause 'che pregano' (who beg/entreat) modifies 'fantolini e vani' and uses the present indicative 'pregano'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g125: The clause begins with coordinating 'e' (and). '’l' is the elided form of 'lo' (the masculine direct object/article) attached to the following noun; here 'l pregato' functions as a substantive past participle 'the one/thing prayed to'. 'non risponde' is present indicative third-person singular 'does not answer/ respond'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g126: 'ma' is the simple adversative conjunction 'but' introducing a contrast with the previous clause.
segments.purgatorio-24-g127: 'per' here expresses purpose ('in order to'). The sequence 'per fare esser' uses 'fare' causatively ('to make') plus the infinitive 'esser' (archaic spelling of 'essere') to mean 'to make [it] be'. 'ben' is an intensifying adverb 'well/fully' modifying the adjective phrase. 'la voglia acuta' is the noun phrase 'the desire' (la voglia) qualified by 'acuta' 'keen/acute' — overall 'in order to make the desire keen'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g128: 'tien' is the present indicative third-person singular of 'tenere' (he/it holds/keeps). 'alto' is a predicate adjective/adverb meaning 'high' or 'held high'. 'lor' is the archaic clitic possessive for 'loro' ('their') modifying 'disio' (an alternate spelling of 'desio' = desire). The second clause 'e nol nasconde' contains 'nol' = 'non + lo' (negative + clitic 'it') meaning 'does not hide it', with 'nasconde' present indicative 'hides/conceals'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g129: 'Poi' = then. 'si partì' is a pronominal past (she departed). 'sì come ricreduta' is a simile/phrase: 'as if/just as (she) changed her mind' — 'ricreduta' is a past participle/adjectival participle (feminine) describing the subject of 'si partì', implying 'she' returned to disbelief or changed her mind.
segments.purgatorio-24-g130: Simple main clause: 'e noi venimmo' = 'and we came/reached'. 'al grande arbore' = 'to the great tree' (the preposition 'a' + article 'il' contracted to 'al'). 'adesso' = 'now/at that moment', placed after the object for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-24-g131: 'che' introduces a relative clause modifying 'arbore'. 'tanti prieghi e lagrime' = 'so many prayers and tears' (tanti quantifies both nouns). 'rifiuta' is a present-tense verb here used in narrative speech: 'refuses/denies' — the tree is personified as refusing those petitions and tears.
segments.purgatorio-24-g132: 'Trapassate' is a plural imperative (addressing multiple people) meaning 'pass through/go past'. 'oltre' reinforces 'beyond'. 'sanza' = archaic 'without'. 'farvi presso' is an infinitive with enclitic pronoun 'vi' (literally 'to make/bring yourselves near'); together 'sanza farvi presso' = 'without approaching/without coming near.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g133: Statement: 'legno è più sù' — 'the wood/tree is higher/up above'. 'che fu morso da Eva' is a relative clause with a reduced/archaic passive: 'fu morso' = 'was bitten' and 'da Eva' marks the agent ('by Eve'). The clause compares this tree to the tree bitten by Eve.
segments.purgatorio-24-g134: Coordination with 'e' (and). 'questa pianta' = 'this plant/tree'. 'si levò' is a pronominal past (si + levò) meaning 'rose up/was raised' — here intransitive 'rose up'. 'da esso' = 'from it' (the comparative tree previously mentioned).
segments.purgatorio-24-g135: 'Sì' = 'thus/so'. 'tra le frasche' = 'among the branches/foliage'. 'non so chi diceva' is a parenthetical admission: 'I don't know who was speaking' with 'non so' (I do not know) + 'chi' (who) + imperfect 'diceva' (was saying/used to say).
segments.purgatorio-24-g136: 'per che' = 'for which reason/wherefore'. The list of travelers is coordinated: 'Virgilio e Stazio e io' = 'Virgil and Statius and I'. The phrase leads into the action they took in consequence.
segments.purgatorio-24-g137: 'ristretti' is a past participle/adjective meaning 'narrowed/pressed together/closely packed' and modifies the three companions (they were close together). It stands alone as a brief participial phrase describing their condition as they moved.
segments.purgatorio-24-g138: 'oltre andavam' = 'we went on farther' (imperfect or past narrative 'andavamo' here used as 'we proceeded'). 'dal lato che si leva' = 'on the side that rises' with relative clause 'che si leva' ('that rises' / 'that lifts up'), describing the direction of their movement.
segments.purgatorio-24-g108: Non (negation) + hanno (present indicative, 3rd pl. of avere) + molto a + infinitive expresses that there is not much (time/opportunity) to perform the action. Volger (infinitive) is the action 'to turn' and quelle ruote is its direct object; word order places the quantified phrase molto before the infinitive construction for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-24-g109: e is the simple conjunction 'and'; drizzò is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of drizzare meaning 'he raised/directed' and supplies the main action of the clause. li occhi is the (archaic) object article + noun (li instead of modern gli), and al ciel is the prepositional phrase 'to heaven/sky' showing the target of the gaze.
segments.purgatorio-24-g110: che introduces a subordinate clause 'that/which'; ti is the indirect object 'to you'; fia is an archaic/fixed future form meaning 'will be' (cf. modern sarà) and takes chiaro as its predicate adjective. Ciò che = 'that which/what' begins a relative clause; 'l mio dir = 'my saying/words' (lo elided to ’l), più modifies dichiarar as 'any longer/further', and non puote is an archaic present 'cannot' (puote = può). The subordinate structure says 'that it will be clear to you what my words can no longer make clear.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g111: Tu is the stressed subject 'you'; ti is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun used with rimanere here (ti rimani = 'you remain/stay'); rimani is present indicative (2nd sg.) of rimanere; omai is an archaic adverb meaning 'now' or 'at this point'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g112: ché is a causal conjunction 'for/because'; ’l is the elided article (il) before tempo; tempo è caro is a copular clause with è (present indicative of essere) and caro as predicate adjective meaning 'precious/valuable' (i.e. limited/important time). in questo regno is a locative phrase 'in this realm/kingdom' explaining where time is precious.
segments.purgatorio-24-g113: sì ch’ (sì che) introduces a result clause 'so that'; io perdo troppo = 'I lose/waste too much' with perdo (present indicative) and troppo 'too much'. Venendo teco is a gerund clause 'by coming with you' giving the circumstance causing the loss. The repeated sì a paro a paro is a doubled prepositional phrase for emphasis meaning roughly 'side by side' or 'step for step' (a paro = 'at the same level/matching'); the repetition heightens the sense of exact, matched pace.
segments.purgatorio-24-g114: Qual here introduces a simile (“just as”); esce is present “comes out/emerges”; alcuna volta = “at some times/occasionally” (volta = time/occasion); di gualoppo = “from a gallop” (gualoppo = gallop); lo cavalier di schiera = “the cavalryman of a troop” (lo + cavalier + di schiera); che cavalchi is a relative clause with cavalchi in the present (here indefinite/poetic) meaning “who rides.”
segments.purgatorio-24-g115: e va per farsi onor del primo intoppo: e = “and”; va is present “he goes/proceeds”; per expresses purpose “in order to”; farsi onor is a reflexive idiom “to make oneself honored / to gain honor” (farsi = make oneself); del primo intoppo = “from/by the first encounter/obstacle” (intoppo = clash/obstacle in context).
segments.purgatorio-24-g116: tal si partì da noi con maggior valchi: tal = “such/so”; si partì is passato remoto with reflexive si “he departed/left”; da noi = “from us”; con maggior valchi = “with greater bounds/strides” (maggior = greater; valchi is literal ‘passes’ but used metaphorically for largeness of steps).
segments.purgatorio-24-g117: e io rimasi in via con esso i due che fuor del mondo sì gran marescalchi: e io rimasi = “and I remained” (rimasi = passato remoto); in via = “on the road”; con esso i due = “with him those two” (esso = him/it used demonstratively); che fuor del mondo = relative clause with fuor (archaic fuor = fuori) “who being out of the world” (i.e. no longer living or apart from the world); sì gran marescalchi = “such great marshals” (sì = so/very, gran = great, marescalchi = marshals — an archaic noun).
segments.purgatorio-24-g118: This is a temporal clause headed by 'quando' (when). 'innanzi a noi' = 'in front of us/before us' and gives the place. 'intrato fue' is an archaic periphrastic perfect: past participle 'intrato' + past tense of essere 'fue' (he had entered / he entered), so it reports a completed past action performed by an implied third-person subject.
segments.purgatorio-24-g119: 'che' introduces a result/explanatory clause. 'li occhi miei' is the subject ('my eyes'). 'si fero' is an archaic/dialectal spelling for 'si fecero' (they made themselves / became): a pronominal past (= became). 'a lui seguaci' means 'followers of him' — 'seguaci' is predicate noun governed by 'si fero' and 'a lui' marks the object of following.
segments.purgatorio-24-g120: 'come' = 'as/just as'. 'la mente' (the mind) is compared to 'si' (the eyes) and is linked to 'a le parole sue' = 'to his words' by the preposition 'a': standard simile structure 'my eyes became followers of him, as the mind [is] to his words'. Word order is normal for a simile.
segments.purgatorio-24-g121: 'parvermi' is a datival perfect meaning 'seemed to me' (verb construction with enclitic 'mi'). 'i rami' is the subject ('the branches') described by the adjectives 'gravidi e vivaci' ('laden/heavy and lively/fresh'). 'd’un altro pomo' is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of another fruit/tree' specifying whose branches.
segments.purgatorio-24-g122: Coordination: 'e' (and). 'non molto lontani' = 'not very far off' (adjectival phrase). 'per esser pur allora vòlto in laci' gives the reason/ circumstance: 'per' + infinitive 'esser' ('for being') + 'pur allora' ('just then') + 'vòlto' (past participle 'turned') + 'in laci' ('into snares/entanglements'). The participle 'vòlto' agrees logically with the branches being turned.
segments.purgatorio-24-g139: Ricordivi is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person plural imperative with the enclitic -vi (= 'vi' = yourselves), addressed to the hearers: 'remember (you all)'. It's set off as the direct command that begins the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-24-g140: dicea is the imperfect indicative of dire (dicea = 'he was saying'), a narrative frame verb that introduces the quoted speech; its imperfect form emphasizes ongoing speech at the time of narration.
segments.purgatorio-24-g141: d’i = di + i (of the); maladetti is a plural adjective/noun 'the accursed ones'; nei = in + i (in the); nuvoli is the poetic plural for 'clouds'; formati is a past participle after the noun, 'formed/fashioned' — together: 'of the accursed formed in the clouds.' Word order places the participle postpositively (typical) and the preposition contracts with the article.
segments.purgatorio-24-g142: che is a relative pronoun ('who/which') introducing the relative clause that follows and referring back to 'i maladetti' (the accursed); it links the description to the action that follows.
segments.purgatorio-24-g143: satolli is an adjective in the plural agreeing with 'maladetti' and means 'sated' or 'full'; placed parenthetically after the relative pronoun it qualifies the accursed before the main verb of the relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-24-g144: Tesëo is the subject (Theseus). Combatter here appears in an archaic/poetic form: an infinitive used narratively where a finite past ('fought') is understood — in context it functions as a past action ('Theseus fought'). co’ is con with apocopation (con → co’ = 'with'), doppi is plural adjective 'double', and petti is the plural noun 'breasts/chest areas' (poetic image, possibly referring to double cuirasses or two breasts).
segments.purgatorio-24-g145: e de li Ebrei = 'and of the Hebrews/Israelites' (de = of/about, li = the). ch’ is the relative 'who/which'; al ber literally 'at the drinking' (al = a + il, here marking the activity of drinking); si mostrar = reflexive construction 'show themselves' (si = reflexive pronoun, mostrar = archaic infinitive 'to show/appear'); molli is plural adjective 'soft/weak/slack.' The whole means 'and of the Hebrews who at drinking showed themselves weak.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g146: per che = a causal/consequential phrase 'wherefore/so that' (here marking consequence). no = negation 'not'; i is the clitic object pronoun 'them' (here representing the Hebrews) placed before the verb; volle is the 3rd-person singular perfect/past of volere ('he would not take/want'); Gedeon is the subject; compagni is a predicate noun 'companions' (he would not take them as companions). Note: the forms are compact: 'so that Gideon would not have them as companions.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g147: quando = 'when'; inver’ is an archaic preposition meaning 'toward' or 'against' (here 'toward/Midian'); Madïan = Midian (place/people); discese is passato remoto/preterite 3rd singular of discendere 'he descended' or 'he went down'; i colli = 'the hills' (object of discese). The clause gives the temporal/place circumstance: 'when he descended the hills toward Midian.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g148: Sì is an adverb meaning 'so' or 'thus' (an intensifier). Accostati is a past participle (masculine plural) agreeing with the implied subject 'we' and expresses an attendant state ('having drawn near'); a + l’un d’i due vivagni is a prepositional phrase 'to one of the two banks' that is the complement of accostati. Passammo is the main verb in the passato remoto ('we passed') and completes the clause.
segments.purgatorio-24-g149: Udendo is a gerund ('hearing'/'as we heard') that gives a simultaneous circumstance to passammo. Colpe is plural feminine 'sins/guilts' and is governed by de la gola ('of the throat'); gola here names the sin (gluttony). Seguite is a past participle agreeing with colpe and means 'followed' or 'ensued'; già is the temporal adverb 'already'; da marks the agent/cause ('by'); miseri qualifes guadagni, 'miserable gains', is the phrase completed by guadagni.
segments.purgatorio-24-g150: Poi is a simple temporal adverb 'then' linking this action to the previous one.
segments.purgatorio-24-g151: Rallargati is a past participle (m. pl.) meaning 'widened again' or 'spread out' and agrees with the implied subject 'we'; per la strada sola is a prepositional phrase 'along the single road' (per = through/along, sola = 'only'/'single' describing strada). The participial phrase modifies the main clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-24-g152: Ben is an emphatic adverb here ('as many as'/'a full'); mille passi e più is the quantified distance ('a thousand steps and more'). Ci is the clitic pronoun 'us' (indirect/object), portar is the infinitive 'to carry/bring' used here with an implied subject ('[the road/they] carried us') in a poetic register, and oltre is an adverb 'further'/'beyond' indicating direction/result.
segments.purgatorio-24-g153: Contemplando is a gerund 'contemplating' showing simultaneous action; ciascun ('each one') refers to each person present; sanza is an archaic form of senza ('without'); parola means 'word'—together 'each one contemplating without a word' (i.e. in silence).
segments.purgatorio-24-g154: Che introduces a direct question here ('what' / effectively 'why'); andate is the present plural 'you go' or 'you walk along'; pensando is a present participle/gerund ('thinking') used with andate to mean 'what are you thinking while you walk'; sì is 'so/thus' (modifying pensando); voi is the 2nd-person plural 'you'; sol is the poetic contraction of solo ('only') placed before tre for emphasis — 'you only three.' Word order is idiomatic and compressed for the verse.
segments.purgatorio-24-g167: 'E' is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this simile to the previous narrative; 'quale' introduces a simile ('such as' / 'like'), preparing the comparison that follows.
segments.purgatorio-24-g168: 'annunziatrice' is a feminine agent noun 'announcer/harbinger' agreeing with the implied subject (the breeze); 'de li albori' is an archaic genitive/partitive ('of the dawns' = of daybreak), with the old plural article 'li' instead of modern 'i'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g169: The phrase 'l’aura di maggio' = 'the May breeze'; 'movesi' is a reflexive present form (moves/stirs itself) and functions as a verb describing the breeze; 'olezza' here is used as a verb-like locus indicating that the breeze gives off smell (i.e. 'breathes fragrance' or 'sends forth an odor').
segments.purgatorio-24-g170: 'tutta' (feminine to agree with 'aura') means 'entirely/wholly'; 'impregnata' is a past participle agreeing with 'aura' and means 'saturated/imbued' (with scent); 'da l’erba e da’ fiori' gives the source/means ('with/from the grass and the flowers').
segments.purgatorio-24-g171: 'tal' ('such') resumes the comparison ('so/thus'); 'mi senti’' is the past tense reporting 'I felt/heard' (here 'I felt'); it is followed by the object 'un vento' ('a wind') and the infinitive clause 'dar per mezza la fronte' where 'dar' ('to give') means 'to strike/offer' and 'per mezza' literally 'through the middle' = 'across the middle of' the forehead ('la fronte').
segments.purgatorio-24-g172: 'e ben senti’ mover la piuma' continues the perception: 'and I well/fairly felt the feather move' — 'ben' intensifies 'senti’' and 'mover' is an infinitive complement to that perception verb, with 'la piuma' as its object.
segments.purgatorio-24-g173: 'che' is a relative conjunction ('which/that') referring back to the motion just described; 'fé' is the archaic past 'fece' (3rd person singular of 'fare') meaning 'caused/made'; it takes the infinitive 'sentir' (to be perceived) with the phrase 'd’ambrosïa l’orezza' = 'the scent/odor of ambrosia' as its object: 'which made the ambrosial scent be perceived.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g174: 'E' again links the report to what comes before; 'senti’ dir' is the idiom 'I heard (someone) say' where 'senti’' (past tense of 'sentire') functions as 'I heard' and is followed by the infinitive 'dir' ('to say'), introducing direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-24-g175: The reported speech opens with 'Beati' ('Blessed [are]') as a proclamation; 'cui' is a relative/dative 'to whom' or 'for whom'; 'alluma' (from 'allumare') is a present (or general) verb meaning 'lights/illuminates/kindles' and takes 'tanto di grazia' ('so much of grace') as its object — 'Blessed are those to whom so much grace gives light.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g176: 'che' introduces the result clause ('so that'); 'l’amor del gusto' = 'the love of taste/pleasure'; 'nel petto lor' = 'in their breast'; 'troppo disir non fuma' is a negative clause where 'troppo' ('too much') modifies 'disir' ('desire') and 'fuma' (literally 'smokes') is used metaphorically: 'their desire does not smoke/burn excessively.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g177: 'esurïendo' is a present participle ('hungry/longing' or 'craving'), here describing the attitude of desire; 'sempre' ('always') stresses continuity; 'quanto è giusto' ('what/that which is right') restricts the craving to what is proper — 'always craving only what is right.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g178: A sentence-final period marking the end of the speech and verse; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-24-g91: A punctuation-only segment: the period closes the preceding speech/line.
segments.purgatorio-24-g92: "Non so" is a simple present indicative: 'I do not know.' The comma and the opening/closing quotes show it's direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-24-g93: "rispuos’ io lui" is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'I replied to him.' The apostrophe indicates a contracted past-tense form (rispuos' ≈ risposi); the order Verb–Subject–IndirectObject is a marked, emphatic word order common in narrative and poetry.
segments.purgatorio-24-g94: "quant’ io mi viva" is an idiomatic clause meaning 'as long as I live.' Quant' is a contraction of quanto/quantoche and viva is an archaic present subjunctive or present form of vivere used idiomatically; mi is the reflexive/emphatic pronoun—together giving 'while I am alive / for as long as I live.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g95: In "ma già non fïa il tornar mio tantosto," ma già = 'but already'; the verb form fïa is an archaic/poetic verbal form used impersonally (here roughly 'would be' / 'will be') and is negated by non; the phrase means 'my return would not be so immediate.' The article il + tornar (infinitive as a noun) = 'the return.' Tantosto = 'so soon' / 'at once.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g96: The subordinate clause introduced by ch' (che) explains the preceding negation: "ch’io non sia col voler prima a la riva." Io non sia is present subjunctive ('that I should not be'); col = con + il ('with the'); voler (infinitive used as a noun) = 'will/wish/intention'; prima a la riva = 'first to the shore.' Together: 'that I should not, of my own will, first be at the shore' — i.e. I would first choose to go to the shore before returning.
segments.purgatorio-24-g97: "però che" functions as 'because' (giving the reason). "’l loco u’ fui a viver posto" = 'the place where I was placed to live' — u' = 'ove/where', fui = past of essere 'I was', a viver posto = 'set/placed to live' (posto is past participle used adjectivally: 'assigned as my place of living').
segments.purgatorio-24-g98: "di giorno in giorno" = 'day by day.' "più di ben si spolpa" uses ben in the sense of 'goods/possessions' and si spolpa is a reflexive/passive-like present ('is flayed/stripped') — literally 'is stripped more and more of its goods day by day.' The si marks the action affecting the place (impersonal/passive sense).
segments.purgatorio-24-g99: "e a trista ruina par disposto" = 'and seems destined for sad ruin.' Par is an archaic form of pare/pare (to appear), and disposto is a past participle used adjectivally ('disposed/arranged/destined'). The whole phrase states the place appears ready/apt for miserable ruin.
segments.purgatorio-24-g100: "Or va" = 'Now go.' Or is an adverb 'now/then'; va is the second-person imperative or present form of andare used here as a direct command—short, colloquial/poetic.
segments.purgatorio-24-g101: "diss’ el" is the contracted past-tense report 'he said' (disse → diss') with el = he; the apostrophe shows the omitted vowel. Word order Verb–Subject again is narrative inversion.
segments.purgatorio-24-g102: The clause "che quei che più n’ha colpa" = 'for the one who has the greater guilt.' The first che functions like 'for'/'because' introducing the clause; quei = 'the one' (the person); the second che = relative 'who'; più = 'more'; n' (ne) = partitive or 'of it/from that' attached to avere: n'ha colpa = 'has of it guilt' → 'has guilt'; colpa = 'fault/guilt/sin.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g103: "vegg’ ïo a coda d’una bestia tratto inver’ la valle ove mai non si scolpa." Vegg' is an archaic first-person present 'I see' (veggio/vedo), here inverted with ïo (I) for emphasis: 'I see [him].' "a coda d’una bestia tratto" uses tratto (past participle of trarre) to mean 'drawn/dragged by the tail of a beast' (a coda = by the tail). Inver' is a contracted form of inver (verso/inver) = 'toward.' "ove mai non si scolpa" = 'where he is never absolved' — si marks the passive/reflexive 'is absolved/exculpated'; mai + non = 'never.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g104: "La bestia ad ogne passo va più ratto" = 'The beast goes faster at every step.' Ad ogne passo = 'at every step' (ogne = poetic form of ogni). Va is present indicative 'goes'; più ratto = 'more swift' (ratto = archaic adjective 'swift/quick').
segments.purgatorio-24-g105: Crescendo is a present participle (gerund-like) of crescere: 'growing/increasing'; sempre = 'always/ever' — together 'ever increasing' or 'growing all the time,' modifying the motion of the beast.
segments.purgatorio-24-g106: "fin ch’ella il percuote" = 'until it strikes him.' Fin (fino) introduces the temporal limit; ch' = che; ella (she/it) refers to the beast; il = him/it (direct object); percuote is present indicative of percuotere 'to strike/beat.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g107: "e lascia il corpo vilmente disfatto." e = 'and'; lascia = present 'leaves' (let/leave behind); il corpo = 'the body' (direct object); vilmente = adverb 'basely/shamefully'; disfatto = past participle 'undone/destroyed' used predicatively — 'and leaves the body shamefully ruined.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g155: A single punctuation mark: a full stop marking the end of the preceding line/utterance. It has no lexical content to parse.
segments.purgatorio-24-g156: "sùbita voce disse;" places the adjective before the noun for a terse, poetic cadence. "sùbita" is a feminine adjective modifying "voce." "disse" is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of dire — "spoke/said." The semicolon links this short report of speech to the consequent reaction.
segments.purgatorio-24-g157: "ond’" is a contraction of onde (therefore/whereupon) introducing the result clause. "io mi scossi" is passato remoto, first person singular, reflexive: "I shook myself/started." The comparison introduced by "come" uses the present plural "fan" (they do) to state a general or habitual behavior: "as frightened beasts and foals do."
segments.purgatorio-24-g158: "Drizzai la testa" is passato remoto (1st person) of drizzare: "I straightened/raised my head." "per veder" is an infinitival purpose clause: "in order to see." "chi fossi" is an indirect interrogative ('who ...') expressed with a subjunctive form; it conveys uncertainty about the person's identity — "to see who he might be." (Note: Dante uses older subjunctive forms and word order for poetic effect.)
segments.purgatorio-24-g159: "e già mai non si videro" is an impersonal/passive construction with "si" plus passato remoto "videro": literally, "and already/ever one did not see" — idiomatically, "never were ... seen." "in fornace" specifies location ("in a furnace"). "vetri o metalli" is the coordinated subject/objects, qualified by the degree adverb "sì" (= so) and the adjectives "lucenti e rossi" (shining and red). The negative cluster yields the sense "never so bright and red."
segments.purgatorio-24-g160: "com’ io vidi un che dicea:" — "com'" (= come) introduces the clause "as I saw someone who was saying." "io vidi" is passato remoto "I saw/witnessed." "un che dicea" (literally "one who was saying") introduces the speech that follows; "dicea" is the imperfect indicative, marking ongoing speech in the past.
segments.purgatorio-24-g161: The direct speech begins: "S’a voi piace montare in sù," an archaic, compressed conditional/conditional-like phrase: "S’a" = "Se a" (if to you). "a voi piace" uses piacere with the indirect object order ("it pleases you"), present indicative "piace." "montare in sù" is an infinitive phrase: "to climb/ascend up."
segments.purgatorio-24-g162: "qui si convien dar volta;" is an impersonal obligation. "qui" = here; "si convien" = it is necessary/fitting (convien = conviene in modern Italian); "dar volta" (infinitive) is a fixed expression meaning "to give a turn/turn about" — so: "here one must turn."
segments.purgatorio-24-g163: "quinci si va chi vuole andar per pace." "quinci" (quinci/quindi) = from here/hence. "si va" impersonal "one goes/one proceeds." "chi vuole andar" = "whoever wishes to go." "per pace" = "for peace" or "in peace" (purpose/condition). The whole sentence: "From here one goes who wishes to go in peace."
segments.purgatorio-24-g164: "L’aspetto suo" places the possessive after the noun (poetic order): "his appearance." "m’avea la vista tolta" uses the compound past in the past perfect sense: imperfect of avere ("avea") + past participle "tolta" = "had taken away (from me)." The participle "tolta" agrees with "vista" (feminine).
segments.purgatorio-24-g165: "per ch’io mi volsi dietro a’ miei dottori," — "per" here introduces a consequence/so-meaning "so/therefore." "ch’io mi volsi" is passato remoto (I turned myself) reflexive: "I turned (around)." "dietro a’ miei dottori" = "toward my teachers/guides."
segments.purgatorio-24-g166: "com’ om che va secondo ch’elli ascolta." "com'" = "come" (like/as). "om" is the archaic form of "uomo" (man/one). "che va secondo" = "who goes/acts according to." "ch’elli ascolta" = "what he hears/listens to," where "elli" is an older pronoun (here functioning as 'he'/'one') and "ascolta" is present indicative "he listens to/hears." The clause compares the speaker's behaviour to a man guided by what he hears.
segments.purgatorio-24-g66: A direct address: O is the vocative exclamation. Frate is a vocative noun meaning “brother” (addressing someone). Word order is the expected exclamation + addressee.
segments.purgatorio-24-g67: Issa is a demonstrative/adverbial/dialectal word here ('now/that'), followed by vegg’ (archaic short for veggio/veggio) meaning 'I see'. Io is the subject pronoun 'I' added for emphasis. The verb form is present and archaic in spelling (vegg’).
segments.purgatorio-24-g68: Diss' is the contracted passato remoto form of dire ('he said'), with the apostrophe marking the dropped -e; elli is an archaic subject pronoun ('he'). This is the reporting clause introducing the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-24-g69: This relative clause contains a noun phrase headed by il nodo ('the knot' / figurative 'crux' or 'tie'), followed by che introducing the relative clause. The sequence 'il Notaro e Guittone e me ritenne' uses ritenne (past tense) meaning 'kept/held back' with a list of objects. 'Di qua dal dolce stil novo' means 'on this side of the dolce stil novo' (di qua = on this side; dal = da + il = 'from the'/'on the side of the'). The final 'ch’ i' odo' is a subordinate relative/causal phrase 'that I hear', with ch' = che and i' = io (the clitic form).
segments.purgatorio-24-g70: Io veggio ben come: Io (I) + veggio (archaic/poetic for 'I see' or 'I perceive') + ben (well) + come (how/as). 'Le vostre penne' is a metaphorical subject ('your pens' = your writers/feathers). 'Di retro al dittator' = 'behind the dictator/leader' (di retro = behind; al = a + il). 'Sen vanno strette' is the verb phrase: 'sen' is a contracted form of 'se ne' (reflexive/partitive particle) + vanno (they go) and strette (tight/close). The whole clause is present indicative and describes current observation.
segments.purgatorio-24-g71: Relative clause introduced by che ('which/that'). 'De le nostre' = 'of ours' (de = di 'of', le nostre = our [pens/styles]). 'Certo non avvenne' uses avvenne (past tense 'happened/occurred') with certo (here an adverb 'certainly') and the negative non — 'this certainly did not happen for ours.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g72: Conjunction e + indefinite relative qual ('who/which/whichever') + più ('more') + a gradire oltre ('to please beyond/any further'); si mette is pronominal 'put oneself' (= 'applies oneself', present tense). The phrase means 'and whoever more tries to please/seek favor beyond (that) sets himself (thus)'. Note gradire here has the sense 'to please/to seek favor'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g73: Negative + verb: non vede più = 'no longer sees/doesn't see anymore.' 'Da l’uno a l’altro stilo' = 'from one style to the other' (da ... a ... indicates range). Stilo is archaic spelling of stile ('style'); here the meaning is literary style. The clause asserts that such a person can no longer distinguish between styles.
segments.purgatorio-24-g74: Simple coordinating conjunction 'e' = 'and', linking to the closing stage of the speech.
segments.purgatorio-24-g75: Quasi here functions as 'almost' or 'as if'; contentato is a past participle used predicatively 'contentato' = 'satisfied/pleased'. Together 'quasi contentato' = 'as if satisfied' or 'almost satisfied.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g76: Si tacette is a pronominal perfect/past action: si (reflexive) + tacette (passato remoto of tacere) = 'he fell silent' / 'he grew quiet'. The reflexive pronoun is part of the verb construction.
segments.purgatorio-24-g77: Simile introducer 'Come' = 'As/like'. 'Li augei' is plural article + poetic word for 'birds'. 'Che vernan' is a relative clause: vernan is an archaic third-person plural 'they come' or present indicative meaning 'that come' (vernal/seasonal coming). 'Lungo 'l Nilo' = 'along the Nile.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g78: Alcuna volta = 'at some times / sometimes'; in aere = 'in the air/sky'. Fanno schiera = 'they form a schiera' (schiera = an array/formation/line). The verb is present indicative describing habitual motion.
segments.purgatorio-24-g79: Poi = then; volan (they fly) più a fretta = 'fly more quickly'; e vanno in filo = 'and go in a line' (in filo = in single-file/line). The verbs are present indicative describing immediate motion.
segments.purgatorio-24-g80: Così tutta la gente che lì era — così ('thus/so') + tutta la gente ('all the people') + che lì era (relative clause 'who were there'). Era is imperfect 'was' describing their state/location.
segments.purgatorio-24-g81: Volgendo is a gerund ('turning') modifying the subject; ’l viso = 'the face' / 'their faces'. The gerund indicates concurrent action ('turning their faces').
segments.purgatorio-24-g82: Raffrettò is passato remoto 'hastened' (here used with la gente as a collective subject), suo passo = 'their pace/step' (suo agrees with la gente as a singular collective). The verb indicates a completed past action in narrative style.
segments.purgatorio-24-g83: E per magrezza e per voler leggera: two causal phrases introduced by per = 'because of/through'. Magrezza = 'thinness/scarcity' and voler leggera = 'a desire to be light/quick' (voler = wanting/wish; leggera = light/nimble). The construction gives two reasons for quickening pace.
segments.purgatorio-24-g84: E come = 'and as'. 'L’uom' (the man) + che di trottare è lasso = relative clause 'who is tired of trotting'. È is the present of essere; lasso = 'weary/tired' (archaic/poetic). Trottare here conveys hurrying along; the whole phrase describes someone who slows down from a fast pace.
segments.purgatorio-24-g85: Lascia + infinitive (andar) = 'he lets go / allows to go' used idiomatically here as 'he leaves/lets his companions go.' Li compagni is the direct object (the companions).
segments.purgatorio-24-g86: E sì passeggia = 'and thus he walks/strolls'; fin che = 'until' + si sfoghi (reflexive verb) = 'it vents/is relieved' + l’affollar del casso = 'the crowding (affollar) of the chest' (del = di + il). Sfoghi here is subjunctival/indicative in dependent temporal clause meaning 'until the pressure/affection is relieved.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g87: Sì lasciò trapassar = 'thus he let (them) pass through' (lasciò + infinitive trapassar = allowed to pass). 'La santa greggia' = 'the holy flock' (greggia = flock). The trailing Forese is the proper name of the person who performed the action (word order places the name after the clause).
segments.purgatorio-24-g88: E dietro meco sen veniva: and (e) + dietro meco = 'behind me/after me' + sen veniva (contracted se ne veniva) = 'he came along' or 'he followed.' Veniva is imperf./past progressive 'was coming' (narrative, he came behind me).
segments.purgatorio-24-g89: Dicendo is a gerund ('saying'), introducing the content of what he said immediately after; it attaches to the preceding clause to report simultaneous speech.
segments.purgatorio-24-g90: An interrogative clause in reported speech. Quando = 'when'; fia is an archaic/future or subjunctive form of essere ('will be' / 'may be' used here in a temporal clause introducing an indirect question); ch'io ti riveggia = 'that I may see you again' with riveggia an archaic verb 'to see again' used in the subjunctive. The whole question reads 'When will it be that I shall see you again?'
segments.purgatorio-24-g36: Vidi is the passato remoto (first person singular) of vedere — 'I saw' (subject 'I' is omitted, as usual). Messer is an honorific placed before a name ("Messer Marchese" = "Sir/ M. Marchese").
segments.purgatorio-24-g37: ch’ is the contracted relative pronoun che = 'who/that' linking back to Marchese. ebbe is passato remoto of avere ('had') and, with spazio, forms the idiom avere spazio di + infinitive = 'have opportunity to'. già here means 'formerly/once'. di introduces the infinitive bere ('to drink'); a Forlì marks the place. con men secchezza literally 'with less dryness' — men is an archaic form of meno ('less') and secchezza means dryness/ reserve of manner.
segments.purgatorio-24-g38: e 'and'. sì is an intensifier 'so'/'thus'. fu is passato remoto (third person singular) of essere = 'he/it was'. tal is an archaic adjective 'such' and prepares the following result clause.
segments.purgatorio-24-g39: che introduces a result clause 'that ...'. non si sentì sazio = 'he did not feel himself sated': sentì is passato remoto of sentire ('felt'/'perceived') used reflexively (si), and sazio means 'sated'/'satisfied'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g40: Ma 'but'. come fa chi guarda = 'as one does who looks'; fa is present of fare 'does/makes'. s’apprezza is reflexive (si apprezza) meaning here 'forms a preference / esteems' or 'is inclined toward' after inspection. più d’un che d’altro is a comparative 'more of one than of another' with di contracted to d'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g41: fei is an archaic past-tense form (poetic passato remoto) of fare — 'I did' / 'I acted'. a quel da Lucca = 'toward that one from Lucca' (a = toward/to; da Lucca marks origin/association: 'of/from Lucca').
segments.purgatorio-24-g42: che introduces a relative clause modifying 'quel da Lucca'. più is comparative 'more'. pare a (parea) is the imperfect 'seemed' (parea = pareva). di me aver contezza is an infinitival phrase: aver contezza 'to have knowledge/awareness' of me — i.e. 'seemed more to know me / be acquainted with me.'
segments.purgatorio-24-g43: El is the subject pronoun 'he' (El in medieval/poetic orthography). mormorava is the imperfect third person singular of mormorare: 'he was murmuring' (ongoing past action).
segments.purgatorio-24-g44: e non so che ... sentiv' io là = 'and I don't know what ... I heard there'. non so is the present 'I don't know'; che here functions as 'what' introducing the quoted word Gentucca; sentiv' is an archaic/clipped imperfect 'sentivo' = 'I heard/ perceived'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g45: ov’ = ov' (where) introduces a clause 'where he felt the wound of justice'. sentia is imperfect 'he felt/perceived'. la piaga de la giustizia = 'the wound/affliction of justice' (piaga = wound/affliction); che sì li pilucca is a relative clause 'which so nibbles/picks at him' — pilucca (piluccare) = 'to pick at/nibble', and li is an older/pronoun form referencing 'him'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g46: O is the vocative particle 'O'/'Oh'. anima is vocative 'soul' addressing the spirit/person directly.
segments.purgatorio-24-g47: diss' io is a contracted poetic form for 'diss' + 'io' = 'I said' (diss' being a past tense form used by Dante, equivalent to dissi/dissi').
segments.purgatorio-24-g48: che par sì vaga di parlar meco = 'who seems so restless/to wander in speaking with me'. par = 'seems' (present), sì is intensifier 'so', vaga (from vagare) here characterizes restless or wandering speech/thought, di + infinitive parlar = 'to speak', meco = 'with me'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g49: fa sì ch'io t'intenda = 'do so that I may understand you'. fa (imperative/causative 'make/do so') + sì ('so that') + ch’ (che 'that') introduces a purpose/result clause; io t'intenda uses the present subjunctive intenda (subjunctive triggered by fa sì che) meaning 'that I may understand you'.
segments.purgatorio-24-g50: e te e me col tuo parlare appaga = 'and satisfy you and me by your speaking'. e te e me lists the two parties 'you and me'; col (con + il) 'with/by the'; tuo parlare = 'your speaking/speech'; appaga is present tense 'satisfies' (here functioning as a jussive/result clause to express the speaker's wish that the speech satisfy both).

sentences.purgatorio-24-s5: Thus he said earlier; and then: "Here it is not forbidden to name each one, since our appearance has been so drained away by the fast."
sentences.purgatorio-24-s6: “This one,” and he pointed with his finger, “is Bonagiunta, Bonagiunta of Lucca; and that face over there from him, more than the others adorned, the Holy Church had in her arms: he came from the Torso, and by fasting he purges (abandons) the eels of Bolsena and the vernaccia.”
sentences.purgatorio-24-s7: Many others he named to me one by one; and at the naming they all seemed content, so that I therefore did not see a gloomy gesture.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s8: I saw, from hunger, use their teeth in vain — Ubaldin of the Pila and Bonifazio, who pastured many people with the crozier.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s13: “A girl has been born, and she is not yet wearing a head-covering,” he began, “who will make my city pleasing to you, however people may judge it.”
sentences.purgatorio-24-s14: You will go away from it with this foreseeing: if in my murmuring you have taken an error, they will still declare to you the true things.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s15: But tell me whether I see here the one who drew forth the nine rhymes, beginning “Women who have understanding of love.”
sentences.purgatorio-24-s16: And I to him: “I am one who, when Love breathes into me, I take note, and in that manner which is dictated within I proceed to explain.”
sentences.purgatorio-24-s1: Neither the saying nor the walking, nor did the walking make him any slower, but while reasoning we went fast, like a ship painted and driven by a good wind; and the shades, which appeared like half-dead things, through the hollows of their eyes drew astonishment from me, aware of my living.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s2: And I, continuing my discourse, said: "She is going up perhaps more slowly than she would (otherwise),
sentences.purgatorio-24-s3: But tell me, if you know, where Piccarda is; tell me whether I can see any person among these people who so concerns me."
sentences.purgatorio-24-s4: "My sister, who between beauty and goodness — I do not know which was greater — rejoices triumphant, already with her crown, in high Olympus."
sentences.purgatorio-24-s29: I saw people beneath it raise their hands and shout something toward the leaves — I know not what — like eager, vain little boys who beg, and the one they beg does not answer; but, to make their longing keen, it keeps their desire held high and does not hide it.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s30: Then she departed, as if changed in mind; and we came to the great tree now, which rejects so many prayers and tears.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s31: "Pass on beyond without coming near: the wood is higher than the one that was bitten by Eve, and this plant rose up from it."
sentences.purgatorio-24-s32: Thus, among the foliage I don't know who was speaking; wherefore Virgil and Statius and I, pressed together, kept going further along the side that rises.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s25: They do not have much time to turn those wheels,» and he lifted his eyes to heaven, «which will make clear to you what my words can no longer explain.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s26: You remain now; for time is precious in this realm, so that I lose too much by coming with you, side by side.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s27: Just as, at times, a cavalryman of a troop will come out of a gallop — a horseman who rides — and goes to gain honor from the first encounter, so he parted from us with larger bounds; and I remained on the way with those two who, being out of the world, were such great marshals.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s28: And when he had gone in ahead of us, my eyes became followers of him, as the mind is to his words; the branches of another fruit seemed to me heavy and fresh, and not very far off, for they had only then been turned into snares.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s33: "Remember," he was saying, "the accursed ones fashioned in the clouds — who, sated, Theseus fought with double breasts; and the Hebrews who at drinking showed themselves weak, so that Gideon would not take them as companions when he descended the hills toward Midian."
sentences.purgatorio-24-s34: So, having drawn close to one of the two banks, we passed on, hearing sins of the throat already followed by miserable gains.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s35: Then, spread out along the single road, we went on for a full thousand steps and more, each one contemplating without a word.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s36: What are you thinking as you walk along, you three alone?
sentences.purgatorio-24-s41: And as, like a harbinger of the dawn, the May breeze stirs and breathes fragrance, wholly soaked with grass and flowers; so I felt a wind strike across my brow, and I clearly felt the feather move, which caused the scent of ambrosia to be perceived.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s42: And I heard it said: "Blessed are those to whom so much grace lights up, that the love of taste in their breast does not let desire smoke too much, always craving only what is right!"
sentences.purgatorio-24-s22: "I do not know," I answered him, "for as long as I live; but my return will not be so soon that I would not, of my own will, first go to the shore; for the place where I was set to live is day by day more and more stripped of its goods, and seems destined for sad ruin."
sentences.purgatorio-24-s23: "Now go," he said; "for the one who is more to blame, I see him dragged by the tail of a beast toward the valley where he is never absolved."
sentences.purgatorio-24-s24: The beast goes swifter at every step, growing ever larger, until it strikes him, and leaves the body shamefully undone.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s38: A sudden voice spoke; whereupon I started, as frightened beasts and foals do.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s39: I raised my head to see who it was; and never in a furnace were glass or metals seen so bright and red, when I saw someone who was saying: "If it pleases you to climb up, here one must turn; from here goes whoever wishes to go in peace."
sentences.purgatorio-24-s40: His appearance had taken my sight away; so I turned back to my teachers, like a man who goes according to what he hears.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s17: “O brother, now I see,” he said, “the knot that held the Notary and Guittone and me on this side of the sweet new style that I hear!”
sentences.purgatorio-24-s18: I see clearly how your pens, trailing behind the dictator, are drawn close — something that certainly did not happen with ours; and whoever sets himself to please further can no longer see from one style to the other; and, as if satisfied, he fell silent.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s19: Like the little birds that come each spring along the Nile, sometimes they form a squadron in the air; then they fly faster and go in line; so all the people who were there, turning their faces, quickened their pace — both because of thinness and because they wanted to move lightly.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s20: And as a man who is weary of trotting leaves his companions and so strolls until the crowding in his chest is relieved, so Forese let the holy flock pass, and he came after me with me, saying: “When will it be that I see you again?”
sentences.purgatorio-24-s9: I saw Messer Marchese, who once had the opportunity to drink in Forlì with less reserve, and was such that he did not feel sated.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s10: But as one does who looks and then prefers one more than another, so I acted toward that one from Lucca, who seemed to know me better.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s11: He was murmuring; and I don't know what — 'Gentucca' — I heard there, where he felt the wound of justice that so picks at him.
sentences.purgatorio-24-s12: 'O soul,' I said, 'who seems so restless to speak with me, do so that I may understand you, and satisfy both you and me by your speaking.'
